title: Simply impactful story telling
review: Haq is a triumph, carried by its best assets: a powerful story and stellar acting. The film's narrative, a gripping courtroom drama based on the Shah Bano case, is handled with rare maturity and restraint. Yami Gautam and Emraan Hashmi deliver career-best performances, playing their roles with raw, nuanced, and extraordinary conviction. A must-watch.

title: Bold and Brilliant Film Inspired by Real Events
review:
Haq is a powerful movie inspired by the real-life case of Shah Bano vs. Mohammad Ahmad Khan (1985) - a landmark judgement that exposed the injustice faced by Muslim women under the triple talaq system. The film beautifully portrays the clash between secular law and Muslim personal law, raising vital questions about gender equality and social reform.

Yami Gautam delivers a deeply moving performance, and Emraan Hashmi - the "King of Love" - adds strength and charm to every scene. The movie courageously highlights the hypocrisy of the judicial system and the resistance to reform within society.

A fantastic, unexpected, and thought-provoking film from Bollywood. Hats off to everyone involved in creating this remarkable piece of cinema.

title: Haq se dekho
review:
I might be a bit late to celebrate this incredible movie, but it's never too late to appreciate one of the best movies to have come out of Bollywood recently. Yami Gautam is an amazing actress who deserves far more recognition and acclaim than she currently receives.

Every scene, every expression she delivers, lands perfectly, capturing the pain and disappointment of a woman who sought justice-not only for herself but for every woman in her community. The movie doesn't have any unnecessary fluff and remains focused and impactful throughout its runtime. It features some of the best courtroom scenes I have seen. Yami Gautam delivers a deeply moving monologue that brings the entire movie full circle. The music serves as a powerful supporting actor, reminding you of the movies core issues.

title: Great movie, Simple yet impactful
review:
I loved this movie , it really hit hard and made me see marriage and women from a completely different perspective. Banu was right. I now understand what dignity truly means to a woman and how important her well-being is, especially when her husband takes a stand or makes a decision, whatever the reason may be.

The film subtly explores themes of patriarchy, social norms, shame, male ego, power, money, authority and even the right to act under religious or cultural expectations as a man. There's something here we, as men and as human beings, need to learn and grow from.

It beautifully portrays how love and marriage can fade over time, leaving a woman isolated despite her immense contribution to the relationship. The feminine perspective is handled with grace and depth, showing both vulnerability and strength.

For me, the best part was the husband's character and Emraan Hashmi's brilliant performance. He played the role with such confidence and portraying the man exactly as he was, without guilt or pretense. His character appears decent and respectable, yet turns from grey to dark when his ego is hurt. His expression of love and care becomes tricky in the second half, shifting toward selfishness and self-preservation as he tries to protect his social image.

His character has real depth, and Emraan Hashmi brought it to life in a believable and layered way. Through him, we see not just a man, but the reflection of how society, ego, and pride can shape love, marriage, and dignity.

title: A must watch movie
review: Haq is a powerful portrayal of the real struggles faced by women in Muslim communities. The film captures their pain and strength with honesty and depth. Both actors have delivered outstanding performances, making this a must-watch movie. I like the movie all should watch it and it will gonna create a positive impact on the society.

title: Masterpiece of Truth, Courage, and Justice
review:
Haq isn't just a movie - it's an experience, a statement, and a revolution in storytelling. Directed by Suparn S Varma, this courtroom drama shines as one of the most powerful and emotionally charged films of 2025.

Story & Direction
Inspired by real events, Haq tells the story of Shazia Bano (Yami Gautam Dhar), a woman who refuses to be silenced after being unjustly divorced through triple talaq. Her fight for justice becomes the voice of millions - turning her pain into power.

Suparn S Varma's direction is flawless - every frame, every line, every silence carries weight. He brings sensitivity and strength together like few directors can.

Performances
Yami Gautam Dhar delivers the performance of a lifetime. She carries the soul of the film - fierce yet vulnerable, determined yet deeply human. Her courtroom scenes will leave you speechless and emotional.

Emraan Hashmi is equally magnetic - layered, complex, and hauntingly real. He plays Abbas Khan with nuance, showing both his love and his flaws.

Their on-screen chemistry adds incredible emotional tension and depth.

Writing & Emotion
The dialogues are piercing and unforgettable - not preachy, but powerful. Every word feels like truth spoken for generations of women who were never heard.

The film blends law, faith, and humanity with grace - reminding us that justice is not just a system, it's a right.

Cinematography & Music
The visuals are breathtaking - capturing both the beauty and the pain of small-town India. The courtroom sequences are shot with raw realism.

The background score elevates every emotion - subtle, stirring, and unforgettable.

Message
Haq is not about one woman - it's about every woman who dares to stand up for herself. It celebrates courage, equality, and truth. The film leaves you proud, teary-eyed, and inspired all at once.

title: Must Watch Movie✨
review:
I really loved this movie...The story is strong and the film keeps you hooked from start to end😍
Yami Gautam is the highlight of the movie✨..Her acting is so powerful that she even overshadows Emraan Hashmi🤧 Every dialogue she delivered gave me goosebumps😭🤧She truly deserves a National Award for this performance✨

Overall, Haq is a must-watch💯

title: Haq Movie - A balanced, powerful, and must-watch film for serious cinema lovers.
review:
I had high hopes for Haq, and honestly, the film didn't disappoint. Based on the real-life Shah Bano case (Mohd. Ahmad Khan vs Shah Bano Begum, 1985), the movie explores a sensitive subject with balance and grace. The makers have taken creative liberty by changing names and some details to avoid hurting religious sentiments, but the essence of the story remains intact.

The first half is strong and engaging, with solid character development that helps the audience connect emotionally. The second half, though not as powerful as expected after the intermission, still holds up well - especially due to Yami Gautam and Emraan Hashmi's brilliant performances. The courtroom drama could've been more impactful, but overall, the execution is decent.

Haq is not a commercial entertainer - it's a serious, content-driven film that sheds light on a real issue. Those who enjoy true-story-based cinema will find it meaningful and thought-provoking. The film ends with the Supreme Court judgment, leaving the post-1985 controversies untouched.

Yami Gautam shines as Shazia Bano, portraying strength and struggle beautifully. It's definitely worth watching for her performance and the story's relevance.

My Rating: 7/10.

title: MIND BLOWING MOVIE
review:
Do watch it, it's absolutely amazing and will capture your attention from start to finish. Some movies you simply watch, but this one, you truly feel. It captures you from the very first scene and never lets go till the end. It's not just a film, it's an experience, a judgment, a truth brought to life with such sincerity that it leaves you speechless.

This story, which we've all studied as a law student and read this landmark case year after year, comes alive here with such emotional power that it feels new, raw, and deeply personal. Every dialogue, every glance, every silence carries meaning. It's the kind of film that reminds you what cinema truly stands for emotion, truth, and connection.

Yami Gautam is absolutely extraordinary, she delivers one of the finest performances of her career. The grace, conviction and quiet power she brings to her character are truly mesmerizing. She doesn't just act, she lives the role. Every glance, every pause, every flicker of emotion feels honest and deeply human. Yami has always been known for her subtlety, but here, she transcends her own brilliance commanding the screen with both strength and sensitivity. Emraan Hashmi, on the other hand, reminds us yet again why he remains one of the most underrated yet immensely talented actors in the industry. His controlled intensity, emotional depth and effortless screen presence make every moment he's on screen unforgettable. There's a calm confidence in his performance, one that draws you in and makes you feel every layer of his character's journey.

Together, Yami and Emraan create a rare kind of cinematic harmony, two performers completely in sync with the story and with each other. Their chemistry isn't built on theatrics or drama, but on authenticity, emotion, and truth. Watching them share the screen feels powerful, real and deeply moving. In a time when so many films chase glamour and noise, this one shines purely through its story, soul, and purpose. It's a film that touches something deep within, the kind of movie you carry in your heart long after it ends.

A standing ovation to the entire team, from the direction to the screenplay, the cinematography and the background score, every element blends seamlessly to create something truly magical. Go watch it. Its worth it...

title: Haq isn't propaganda - it's a wake-up call
review:
After watching Haq, I was deeply moved. The film is not a propaganda piece, but a sincere attempt to awaken thought - to remind us that the very first word revealed in the Holy Qur'an is "Read."

It made me think: how many among us, who claim to fear Allah and follow the path of the Prophet (peace be upon him), truly read and understand this beautiful Book? Too often, we rely blindly on what certain clerics or preachers tell us, without questioning whether their interpretations are truly aligned with the Qur'an or with their own personal motives.

Islam is a faith of knowledge, reflection, and compassion. Yet over time, it pains me to see how its essence has been overshadowed by division and dogma - often by those within our own community who twist the message to serve their own narratives. Anyone who dares to question or seek understanding is quickly labelled as "anti-Muslim" or "influenced by outsiders," instead of being encouraged to seek the truth themselves.

Haq reminded me of the story of Shah Bano - a real woman, a symbol of justice and courage - beautifully portrayed by Yami Gautam. I'm grateful to the filmmakers and artists who presented this story with honesty and depth.

To my fellow believers: let's return to the essence of Islam - to read, reflect, and understand, not just to follow blindly. The Qur'an was revealed as guidance for all humanity, not as a tool for control or fear.

May we all strive to rediscover the true beauty of our faith.

title: I may be slightly biased (the brilliant associate director) Manya Mirchandani , happens to be my neice !) - but Haq completely had me.
review:
Haq is one of those rare films that balances heart and craft with effortless grace. I watched it with a critical eye, ready to pick apart scenes to discuss with Manya - but truthfully, I couldn't find a single thing to critique.

Beautifully shot, elegantly told, and emotionally resonant - it feels like every frame was made with care. Yami Gautam delivers a stellar performance, both subtle and powerful, supported by an ensemble that fits perfectly into the film's mood and message.

A special salute to Director Suparn Verma, his storytelling feels mature, layered, and deeply human. Every emotion lands with quiet precision, and he brings out the best in both his cast and crew.

Cinematographer Pratham deserves high praise -: The lighting, framing, and visual rhythm are exceptional. The film's tone, dialogue, and design blend beautifully, creating something that feels both cinematic and intimate.

And yes, I might be a touch biased - Associate Director Manya Mirchandani, my neice , had a major hand in this creation , but Haq stands tall entirely on its own merit.

If films were graded, this one's straight A++. A warm, heartfelt watch I am going to rewatch .

title: Excellent Movie.
review:
Haq (2025), directed by Suparn Varma and inspired by the landmark Shah Bano case, is not just a film, it is an emotionally searing, intellectually robust, and impeccably executed piece of cinema that commands attention and demands discussion. It lives up to the promise of its title, asserting the fundamental right to dignity and justice with unwavering conviction.

The core of the film's immense power lies in the fabulous acting by Yami Gautam Dhar. As Shazia Bano, a woman fighting for her rights and dignity against a system rigged against her, Gautam delivers a career-defining performance. Her portrayal is a masterclass in controlled intensity, she is fiercely resolute yet heartbreakingly vulnerable. The quiet rage and unwavering strength she channels, particularly during the explosive courtroom monologues, is magnetic and undoubtedly award winning.

Matching her note for note is Emraan Hashmi, who sheds any semblance of conventional villainy to play the entitled and subtly terrifying lawyer, Abbas Khan. Hashmi's nuanced performance is equally award winning, portraying a man whose entitlement, not malice, turns him into a symbol of patriarchal resistance.

The ensemble cast, including the steady support from Sheeba Chaddha and Danish Husain, ensures that every character is grounded, making the legal and emotional conflict intensely real.

The best screenplay by Reshu Nath is the film's backbone. It handles the volatile subject of personal law, triple talaq, and gender justice with maturity, restraint, and exceptional clarity, avoiding preachy melodrama. The dialogues are sharp, authentic, and loaded with emotional weight, eliciting powerful reactions. What emerges is a definitely award winning movie experience that is cohesive, purposeful, and deeply moving.

Finally, the apt music and songs by Vishal Mishra serve the narrative perfectly. The score never overshadows the emotional gravity of the scenes but instead adds necessary texture and weight, ensuring the story's resonance lingers long after the credits roll.

Haq is a courageous, relevant, and flawlessly performed courtroom drama. It is essential viewing that reminds us that the fight for dignity is timeless.

title: Good Movie
review: Haq" is a powerful courtroom drama that engages both the mind and the heart. Written by Reshu Nath and inspired by journalist Jigna Vora's book Bano: Bharat Ki Beti, the film presents its story with honesty and depth. It blends real-life events with creative storytelling in a balanced and believable way. "Haq" stands out as a fine example of meaningful and well-crafted cinema.

title: Highly Recommended 👍🏼
review:
Haq is a worth watching for those who crave intelligent and emotionally resonant cinema. Haq delivers justice not just to its story, but to the power of good filmmaking. Written by Reshu Nath and inspired by journalist Jigna Vora's book Bano: Bharat Ki Beti, the film is a compelling courtroom drama that walks a fine line between reality and creative adaptation with surprising finesse.

The screenplay is the backbone of Haq. It is gripping, particularly in the investigative and emotional sequences. The writing is sharp, observant, and thoughtful, capturing not just the external battle that the protagonist faces, but also the internal turmoil that comes with being misjudged, misrepresented, and wronged by the system. However, there are instances where the pace falters. A few scenes in the mid-portion stretch a little longer than needed, slowing the narrative momentum briefly. Yet, the dialogues remain consistently powerful, pointed, evocative, and often the film's biggest strength.

Suparn S Varma's direction shines in its clarity & restraint, and it complements the writing superbly. His control over the tone of the film is commendable, it never slips into melodrama even when dealing with emotionally charged moments. The first half is particularly engaging, setting up the conflict, characters, and stakes with clarity and intrigue. The second half brings the courtroom into focus, and this is where Varma's direction shines. The arguments, confrontations, and emotional explosions are handled with precision.

Yami Gautam once again proves that she is in a remarkable phase of her career. After Article 370 (2024), she delivers another deeply immersive performance. As Shazia, she embodies vulnerability and strength in equal measure, commanding the screen with precision. Emraan Hashmi is equally compelling and delivers one of his most measured performances in recent years. Vartika Singh delivers a fair performance, fulfilling her role effectively, even if the character itself offers limited range. Danish Husain brings layered maturity. Sheeba Chadha is reliably impactful as always. Aseem Hattangady leaves a noticeable mark. Each supporting performance contributes to building the film's emotional and narrative tension.

Direction 3.5/5
Acting 3.5/5
Music 3/5
Dialogues 4/5
Screenplay 3.5/5.

title: Graceful, Grounded, and Glorious

The soundtrack is gentle, almost invisible, but always there to guide emotion. By the end, I was teary-eyed, not because the film asked me to be, but because it felt so real.

review:
Haq is not just a movie - it's an eye-opener. I truly loved how it explained our Constitution and the idea of the Uniform Civil Code (UCC) in such a simple yet powerful way. The film beautifully connects faith, law, and humanity - showing that all three can coexist with respect. It made me think deeply about what real equality means in our country. Truly a movie that deserves to be seen by everyone.

It encourages you to think and reflect on what equality and justice truly mean in a diverse country like ours. The performances, writing, and direction all come together to make this an important film that everyone should watch at least once.

title: Yami Gautam Proves Her Worth More Than the Film Proves the Worth of Muslim Wives
review:
Haq (2025) :
Movie Review -

I'll tell you an interesting thing about this film - it ends with text information about the Triple Talaq case that took almost three decades to become law. Ironically, the actual film starts right there and ends within 20 seconds when that text fades out. Just before that, the climax shows Shah Bano winning the case of maintenance for her children at the Supreme Court, and I wish that had been the interval point or a pre-climax portion. Suparn Verma makes the same mistake he made earlier in The Trial. In that web series, most of the key moments - the arguments, conspiracies, and verdicts - took place inside the courtroom. Reversing that, Haq takes some of those drawing room, society scenes to the courtroom. You can notice a clear change in the style of arguments as the case travels from the session court to the high court and then to the Supreme Court. By the final stage, the legal arguments and evidence take a backseat, while issues like minority identity, secularism, religious science, and the existence of Muslim traditions come to the front. I understand that shift - it helps in dramatic elevation - but I don't think a courtroom drama should rely more on sentiments than on legal arrangements.

The story revolves around Shah Bano (Yami Gautam), a young woman who falls in love with Advocate Mohammad Khan (Emraan Hashmi). They get married, and nine years later, Mohammad brings another woman into their house. It turns out that he had loved this second wife even before marrying Shah Bano. Feeling humiliated, Bano leaves the house with her three children, still hoping Mohammad will help with their education. But after some time, he stops sending any money. That's when Bano decides to return - to claim her Haq. The matter goes to court, and Bano is protected by IPC 125, which allows her maintenance and alimony for her children. Mohammad doesn't stop there. He divorces her by saying "Talaq" three times, following the Muslim ritual, leaving Bano completely shattered. She then decides to fight the male dominance and outdated traditions that have made the lives of many Muslim women miserable. Now, it's not just her case - it's the case of every Muslim woman. The question is: will she be able to fight this patriarchal system and reclaim her right?

Haq keeps you busy from start to finish and barely gives you a moment to check your phones, except for one song in the second half. The conversations, conflicts, and the portrayal of systematic pressure feel natural, effectively defining this inspiring story based on true events. But just like OMG 2, Haq too fails to grasp the essence of what a true courtroom drama should be. I'm not expecting it to be another 12 Angry Men, Judgment at Nuremberg, A Few Good Men, or even Court (2014) - those are classics known for their confrontations, factual arguments, and layered details. I get it, this is a mainstream Hindi film, not an arthouse project. Still, a film set in the Supreme Court should show at least a bit of logical structure driving the verdict.

For example, there's a scene where Yami Gautam's character goes to the Muslim board to argue her case but ends up walking out defeated despite proving her point. I didn't quite get how that mattered to the ongoing high-court case or the overall story. Why would she or her father walk out as if they've already won something? The bigger point in that scene - that there wasn't a single woman on the board - was a crucial issue, yet the film doesn't make that clear until much later. Only when she reaches the Supreme Court does she speak directly about equality, by which time the focus has shifted from her personal struggle to a broader commentary on the community's existence. Emraan Hashmi's character, in one scene, gives a long speech before the Chief Justice that turns a domestic matter into a community threat. Strangely, no one in the courtroom objects to that irrelevant diversion. The same pattern repeats with Yami's character - she delivers a powerful, emotional speech filled with passion, but there's barely any legal redefining in it. That's where the film falls short. Otherwise, it's a gripping, emotionally engaging story built around an important topic. Another issue is the film's timeline. It ends in 1985, and we're watching it in 2025. That naturally lessens the impact of its historical urgency. The topic of triple talaq was indeed highly controversial even a decade ago, but the heat isn't as intense now. The social and legal environment has evolved, so the film feels slightly late to make a groundbreaking statement.

Now, coming to Yami Gautam - she's simply amazing. In one word, phenomenal. She has delivered yet another award-worthy performance, and I'm sure she'll be ignored once again by those Pan Masala-sponsored award shows and lobby-driven ceremonies. But honestly, there's nothing to be sad about, Yami ma'am. You've won hearts yet again, and there's no denying that you're among the best actors of our times. She completely outshines everyone else in the film - even Emraan Hashmi and, I'd say, the film itself. It's refreshing to see Emraan in such a sophisticatedly villainous avatar, layered yet natural. He doesn't overplay the role and goes along with the flow. He did fine, I guess. Sheeba Chaddha, as always, brings her quiet magic. The moment she appears on screen, you just know she's going to play a character you can't dislike. I only wish Verma had given her a couple of strong monologues. He made a similar mistake in The Trial - along with Sheeba, some of his best actors, were underutilized. Vartika Singh, Paridhi Sharma, Danish Hussain, S. M. Zaheer, Rahul Mittra, and others do their parts without any major flaws. They all help maintain the tone and pace, even if their roles don't stand out individually.

Technically, though, I have a couple of complaints - mainly about the cinematography and sound design. These two aspects are crucial in courtroom dramas. Look at Judgment at Nuremberg again - while it's remembered for its strong courtroom writing, its cinematography, especially in the final sequences, leaves a haunting, unforgettable impact. Those camera angles, those frames - they become part of the storytelling. Haq had the same potential to create a cinematic masterpiece moment, but maybe that's too much to expect from Suparn Verma, who, frankly, isn't Stanley Kramer. The editing is fine, though. It's crisp enough to keep you engaged without unnecessary drag. The production design serves its purpose - the courtrooms, the houses, and the old-era settings all look authentic.

Suparn Verma's storytelling does keep you invested. He builds cinematic tension and makes you feel genuine anger against the arrogance of the male-dominated Muslim community and especially the tradition of triple talaq. For a while, you feel that frustration deeply. But right after that emotional peak, you also realize that it's India of the 1970s and '80s that's being shown. In today's context, where laws and rights have improved significantly, that anger naturally softens into reflection. So yes, applause to Verma for creating that emotional pull and cinematic tension - and hats off to Yami Gautam for empowering his vision with her strength and acting finesse. Haq may not be a perfect courtroom drama, but it's certainly an important film. It's important for the Muslim community, both men and women, as it revisits a historical struggle that shaped modern personal laws. But I'm not sure if the larger audience - the majority - will connect to it with the same intensity. Still, it's a film worth watching. It may not redefine cinema or courtroom dramas, but it reopens a conversation we tend to forget. And that, in itself, is an achievement. Overall, Haq is a watchable, thought-provoking drama driven by a stellar Yami Gautam, a decent Emraan Hashmi, and a director who knows the emotion but not always the craft. It's flawed but honest, dated but meaningful, and above all, a reminder of how one woman's fight for dignity can echo through generations.

RATING - 5/10*

title: Bold, But Not Pathbreaking!
review:
The Quiet Revolution: What Works

Suparn S. Varma's Haq announces itself as cinema with conviction. This courtroom drama based on the landmark 1985 Shah Bano case arrives at a moment when mainstream Bollywood hesitates to engage with contentious social realities. The film's boldness lies not in shouting, but in speaking clearly on a subject that could have easily spiraled into incendiary melodrama or reductive moralizing.

The film's greatest achievement is its restraint. It handles the volatile intersection of religious law, personal rights, and constitutional justice with a maturity that borders on artistic grace. Rather than positioning its narrative as a crusade against religion or tradition, Haq presents a deeply human conflict where no character is purely villainous and no ideology entirely righteous.

Performances That Transcend Script

Yami Gautam Dhar delivers what may be her career-best performance as Shazia Bano. Her transformation from a reserved, vulnerable housewife to a determined crusader for dignity is executed with remarkable nuance. What distinguishes her portrayal is its absence of melodrama. She does not suffer theatrically; instead, she accumulates the weight of microaggressions and broken promises until dignity becomes the only rational response. In the film's climactic moments, her quiet conviction carries more force than any passionate outburst could.

Emraan Hashmi, as Abbas Khan, brings unexpected depth to what could have been a one-dimensional antagonist. His performance layers complexity into a character who appears decent and respectable on the surface yet reveals his true nature-a man whose love and care shift toward self-preservation when his ego is wounded. Through Hashmi's measured restraint, the audience glimpses how society, pride, and personal insecurity can calcify into injustice. The verbal duels between Gautam and Hashmi in the courtroom sequences are electric, with dialogues that land like thunder and arguments that pierce the narrative's emotional core.

Language as Emotional Architecture

The film's screenplay by Reshu Nath deserves particular recognition. The dialogue blends classical Urdu-inflected Hindi with sudden English flourishes, creating a linguistic texture that feels both authentic and cinematic. What makes the writing exceptional is its economy-every exchange carries thematic weight. Lines are not delivered; they are weaponized in arguments about dignity, rights, and interpretation of sacred law.

Vishal Mishra's music, composed alongside lyricist Kaushal Kishore, amplifies this emotional architecture. Songs like "Dil Tod Gaya Tu" capture the ache of betrayal with soulful minimalism, while "Haq Hai Mera" functions as an empowering anthem without sacrificing emotional honesty. The music does not overshadow the narrative; it inhabits it, serving as a second voice in the film's conversation about loss, resilience, and self-worth.

Where the Film Falters: The Courtroom Drama That Could Have Been

Yet here lies the film's central limitation. Haq announces itself as a courtroom drama, and while its courtroom sequences are undoubtedly the film's high points-fierce, charged with ideological collision-the film ultimately does not commit to the form it appears to promise. The courtroom scenes, despite their intensity, function primarily as emotional crescendos rather than as genuine legal proceedings.

The original Shah Bano case spanned nearly seven years across multiple court levels, from the Session Court in Indore through the Madhya Pradesh High Court to the Supreme Court. The case involved intricate legal arguments about the secular nature of Section 125 of the Criminal Procedure Code, the applicability of civil law to Muslim personal matters, and the government's contested interpretation of Islamic law regarding maintenance. Shah Bano, a 62-year-old woman married for 43 years, was left with merely INR200 monthly maintenance after triple talaq before fighting her way to a landmark judgment that redefined rights for Muslim women across India.

Haq condenses and abstracts these elements into a more intimate family drama. While the film's creative liberties are understandable-it is cinema, not a documentary-the condensation costs the narrative significant weight. The Supreme Court hearing, in particular, is wrapped up through rousing speeches and closing statements rather than the detailed examination of legal principle that animated the historical case. The courtroom becomes a stage for emotional catharsis rather than a battleground of ideas, where the intricacies of law and its interpretation could have created pathbreaking cinema.

The Missing Layers: Factual Fidelity and Comprehensive Storytelling

The film's decision to change names and alter specific details was made, by the filmmakers' account, to avoid controversy. This choice itself reveals the film's central compromise. By softening the historical record, Haq gains emotional universality but sacrifices the specificity that would have made it a definitive artistic statement on a particular moment in Indian legal history.

The real Shah Bano case was not merely a personal tragedy; it was a constitutional crisis. The Supreme Court's 1985 judgment sparked nationwide protests, prompted the Rajiv Gandhi government to pass the Muslim Women (Protection of Rights on Divorce) Act, 1986-legislation that many women's rights advocates saw as a betrayal-and positioned Shah Bano herself as an unwilling symbol whose later public distancing from the verdict added another tragic layer. These broader implications, the political machinery that moved behind the judicial decision, and the ultimate compromise that undermined the judgment's promise remain largely absent from Haq.

The film also simplifies the moral terrain. While its refusal to demonize Islam or Muslims is commendable, it leaves unexplored the ways in which institutional religious authority (represented in the film through the Muslim Law Board) can perpetuate harm. The opportunity to examine how interpretations of sacred law become tools of control, and how reform within religious traditions requires both internal and external pressure, largely slips away.

The Verdict: Bold, But Not Pathbreaking

Haq is a film that demands respect. In an era when cinema increasingly retreats from substantive engagement with social reality, a director willing to examine gender, religion, law, and justice with this level of earnestness is rare. The performances are exceptional, the dialogue sharp, the music soulful, and the film's treatment of its subject is remarkably sensitive.

But the film remains a portrait of a moment rather than a complete examination of it. It is a powerful character study that happens to be framed as a courtroom drama, rather than a courtroom drama that uses character to illuminate law. It could have been pathbreaking-the kind of film that doesn't just entertain or move audiences but fundamentally alters how cinema engages with constitutional questions and women's rights. Instead, it accomplishes something more modest but still valuable: it opens a conversation and does so with grace, nuance, and performances that linger.

title: Nice 🎥
review:
Every Scene Feels Honest

title: Extremely too long for this screenplay
review:
Foreign-language dramas about family turmoil often carry a certain emotional potency, but HAQ feels uniquely conflicted in how it approaches its core subject of divorce. The film isn't terrible by any stretch, yet it's also nowhere near strong enough to justify how narrowly and relentlessly it clings to its single thematic thread. At its best, the movie shows flashes of sincerity, a few compelling moments of human frailty, and a cast that genuinely tries to elevate the material. But at its worst, it becomes a long, slow meditation on one idea-divorce-and never expands beyond that, leaving the audience with a sense of emotional repetition instead of emotional revelation.

The screenplay is the clearest culprit here. Instead of exploring divorce through layered perspectives, enriched subplots, or even unexpected dramatic pivots, the writing plays out in a straight line. It's competent but never bold, and while the foundation is solid enough, there's no attempt to deepen or evolve the narrative as it unfolds. The script feels like it was written for a very specific audience-almost like a private fable or a cautionary bedtime story-rather than for a general crowd looking for nuance, engagement, or cinematic weight. It's "okay," but "okay" hardly sustains a feature-length film when the emotional toolbox is this limited.

The direction mirrors those same limitations. For a story dealing with heartbreak, separation, and emotional reconstruction, the visuals and pacing feel surprisingly muted. Cinematic moments that should land with resonance instead drift by without emphasis, as if the director never fully committed to shaping an emotional arc. There are scenes that clearly should have held more weight, yet they appear in the final cut feeling flat or unfinished, depriving the audience of the impact the subject matter deserves. With better staging and a more dynamic approach, many of the film's quieter moments could have felt profound instead of simply slow.

Still, HAQ isn't without strengths. The costumes are genuinely impressive, adding a sense of cultural vibrancy and texture that the screenplay fails to deliver. And the cast delivers earnest, grounded performances that often make the characters more compelling than the writing gives them permission to be. Their work is the main reason the movie remains watchable at all; without them, the film's thinner moments would collapse entirely.

Ultimately, HAQ lands somewhere in the middle-too competent to dismiss, too limited to praise. It's one of those films you can sit through once and appreciate for its effort and craft, but it gives you absolutely no incentive to revisit it. With a stronger script and more imaginative direction, it could have been something resonant and culturally rich. Instead, it settles for being a long, modestly effective meditation on divorce that never quite reaches the emotional heights it aims for.

title: A Powerful Reflection on Marriage, Ego, and Equality
review:
Based on true events and an iconic chapter from Indian history (The Shah Bano Case), Haq addresses an important and often overlooked topic - marriage, divorce, and alimony.

Without getting into the political or religious intricacies, I'd like to talk about it purely from a film and story perspective.

A happy marriage comes crashing down due to promises disguised as good deeds and obligations - reflecting the role of a woman who is seen only as a mother and caretaker, without having a say in decisions that are equally important for her own life and family.

As the drama unfolds, the unfortunate situation worsens, showcasing how a man takes his wife's feelings into no consideration, fails to acknowledge his own wrongdoings because of his ego, and refuses to apologise.

Instead, he looks at himself as superior and claims her to be in the wrong, justifying his actions as favours or good deeds.

What follows is a battle of egos and dignity, with every decision further escalating the drama at each step.

The film also reflects on society's outlook - one that deems such situations normal, views marriage as a contract, divorce as taboo, and rarely stands by a woman during her toughest times.

It highlights how often a woman is deemed the culprit, without considering the emotional and personal toll it takes on her. Yet, the strength and resilience she carries forward is truly admirable.

A deeply emotional and inspiring watch, Haq is a story carried beautifully by strong writing, powerful dialogues, and a gripping screenplay - complemented by equally impactful performances.

Yami Gautam, in particular, delivers a standout act that makes you feel and connect not just with her struggle and personal journey, but with every woman who has ever faced such injustice.

And as mentioned earlier, keeping all religious and political sentiments aside, Haq serves as a reminder of a sad reality that continues to be neglected - one where a woman's perspective is often unheard or overlooked.

title: The movie made me speechless
review: This movie addresses a deeply complicated issue, portraying the injustice Muslim women faced through 'triple talaq' -a controversial practice often attributed to historical misinterpretations of the law. As a Muslim man, imagining myself in Shazia Bano's position, I Couldn't stop my tears. The film poignantly depicts the plight of women in 1980s India. When the Supreme Court judges finally delivered their verdict, I literally got goosebumps. The storytelling is exceptional, and Emraan Hashmi and Yami Gautam perfectly nailed their characters.

title: Good idea ..bad execution
review:
Haq is often mistaken as being inspired by the Shah Bano case-but that assumption is completely wrong. This is not Shah Bano's story. The film follows a different premise altogether, and unfortunately, it fails to justify its own narrative.

The biggest issue is the lack of emotional depth. There is no soul in the drama, no intensity in the conflicts. Everything feels staged and artificial. Instead of realism, the film overloads itself with songs in almost every situation, which only weakens the impact of serious moments.

The detailing is careless. Even the Nikah scene is inaccurately shown, breaking cultural authenticity and exposing weak research. Polygamy is a lived reality for many, filled with emotional complexity, but the film never captures that pain or tension.

The idea itself is relevant, but execution is poor. Direction feels weak and emotionally disconnected, as if the filmmaker never truly felt the story from within. A potentially powerful subject ends up as a shallow, poorly crafted film.

Haq is not a bad idea-it is a badly made film.

title: Haq is a strong and important movie, and I highly recommend it.
review:
Shazia Bano, played by Yami Gautam, marries Abbas Khan, Emraan Hashmi. They have a happy marriage, two children and a third on the way. Then Abbas grows distant and suddenly leaves for Pakistan. When he returns three months later, he brings back a second wife for Shazia. Shocked and angry at first, Shazia slowly forces herself to accept that Abbas is now married to two women.

Everything changes when the second wife, Saira, played by Vartika Singh, reveals that Abbas was already in a relationship before marrying Shazia. When Shazia confronts him about the lies that brought Saira into their home, Abbas responds by divorcing her on the spot. Shazia refuses to accept this and decides to fight back.

Supported by her father, Shazia takes the matter to court to defend her rights as a wife and her children's right to support. Her biggest obstacle is Abbas himself. He is a trained lawyer who knows the system and the men who control it. The more Shazia commits to the case, the deeper she sinks. Like standing in quicksand, every step forward makes it harder to escape. She begins to doubt herself. The Muslim community turns against her family, support from relatives disappears and the situation becomes darker and more dangerous for both her and her children.

Haq is not a typical film about a woman fighting for her rights. It is about religion, power and living in a community that fails its own people. It challenges traditions and rules created and protected by men. The soundtrack is spot on and knows exactly when to lift the emotion and when to step back and let scenes breathe. The acting is phenomenal. I have always liked Emraan Hashmi as an actor and he delivers here. Still, this is Yami Gautam's film. She carries the story completely, moving through anger, fear, strength and despair with impressive control.

I do have a few minor notes. As a Hindu, I did not always fully understand the Muslim customs and religious aspects that are closely tied to the case. Some of those conversations moved too fast for me to fully grasp their weight. That said, it did not affect my rating. Sadly, my sister went through a very similar legal battle a decade ago while fighting for her three children. We even lost our father during that time. Watching this brought back memories, but it only reinforced how honest this film feels.

title: A Deeply Moving Story of Faith, Rights, and Resilience !!!
review:
'Haq' tells an extremely relevant story, one that feels urgent and necessary. Inspired by true events, the film is a fictionalized and dramatized version of the story stated in the book Bano: Bharat Ki Beti, written by Jigna Vora. The film is an emotionally charged retelling of the historic Shah Bano case that shook India in the 1980s, and it traces the controversial maintenance lawsuit where the Supreme Court ruled in favour of divorced Muslim women. Suparn S Varma's vision is clear-he wants to remind us that personal stories can often echo profound social truths. The result is a film that feels both urgent and deeply affecting. With Yami Gautam and Emraan Hashmi leading from the front, 'Haq' becomes a rare social drama that manages to stay engaging without ever turning preachy. It's a story crafted with restraint, empathy, and a quiet sense of purpose.

Set in the 70s and 80s, this period film revolves around Shazia Bano (Yami Gautam Dhar), who lives in Aligarh with her husband, Abbas Khan (Emraan Hashmi), a well-respected lawyer. After three children and years of marriage, her life shatters when Abbas returns from Pakistan with his second wife, Saira (Vartika Singh). The humiliation that follows winds up in divorce after Abbas pronounces the three irrevocable words-talaq, talaq, talaq. Struggling to survive with even the bare minimum financial support withdrawn, Shazia approaches the courts for justice, supported strongly by her father, Maulvi Basheer (Danish Husain), who remains steadfast by her side. When the case reaches the Supreme Court, it becomes not only a legal battle but also a moral one that reflects the struggle of countless women across the country. Every scene feels grounded and real, drawing you into Shazia's world of quiet defiance and resilience.

At its core, 'Haq' is a simple story told with sincerity-and that simplicity becomes its biggest triumph. The narrative never overcomplicates itself or resorts to unnecessary drama, unfolding in a linear and honest way. The director anchors the storytelling in emotion, never losing sight of the human cost beneath the legal and ideological battles. Much of the credit goes to Reshu Nath, whose writing-spanning story, dialogue, and screenplay-exudes clarity and cohesion. The film beautifully captures the texture of Western Uttar Pradesh, with dialogues that sound authentic and grounded in the soil. Every frame and line carries intention, resulting in an experience that's both immersive and emotionally resonant.

'Haq' also thrives on its performances. Yami Gautam delivers one of her finest acts yet-she embodies Shazia's pain, courage, and dignity with remarkable control. She dominates every frame, delivering a performance that's understated and unforgettable. Emraan Hashmi matches her beautifully, portraying Abbas with restraint and moral ambiguity instead of overt villainy. His anger is quiet, and that subtlety works. Look for the courtroom exchange toward the climax, where Emraan and Yami's characters face the Supreme Court judges and lay bare their beliefs with raw intensity. Vartika Singh as Saira is pleasant but somewhat underutilized, while Danish Husain as Maulvi Basheer brings compassion to the story. Sheeba Chaddha, as Bela, her steadfast lawyer, adds depth and gravitas to every scene she's in. Together, the ensemble ensures that every emotional note leaves an impact.

It takes courage to make a film like 'Haq' in an era when action spectacles, horror, and comedies dominate the screens. Suparn S Varma directs it with unwavering conviction, turning what could have been a niche courtroom drama into a universal story about justice, faith, and dignity. Vishal Mishra's songs may not linger long after the credits, but they serve the story well and add emotional texture. What truly sets 'Haq' apart is its refusal to confine itself to a community-specific narrative; instead, it emerges as a story of betrayal, resilience, and hope. It stands tall as a film that speaks for the voiceless, upholds its integrity, and reminds us that truth, when expressed with simplicity, can be the most potent form of protest.

title: A Reflection of Inner Strength
review:
A Reflection of Inner Strength
Haq celebrates the power of quiet resilience. It's not about shouting for justice, it's about standing firm in truth. Yami Gautam's portrayal of Shazia is one of the most graceful performances I've seen in a long time. Emraan Hashmi's controlled emotions add richness to the narrative. The film's tone is calm, almost meditative, yet the emotions are intense. It's a reminder that strength doesn't always roar - sometimes, it simply endures.

title: Propaganda against Islam. Purely politically motivated and malicious.
review:
Haq presents itself as a courtroom drama rooted in justice, women's rights, and constitutional values. However, beneath its emotional storytelling and polished performances lies a narrative that many viewers perceive as politically motivated and selectively constructed, raising serious concerns about the spread of disinformation through cinema.

The film claims to be inspired by real events, yet it simplifies and reshapes complex historical, legal, and social realities to fit a predetermined ideological framework. By isolating specific aspects of the case it draws from, Haq creates a one-sided moral narrative, presenting certain communities and traditions as inherently regressive while positioning the state and judiciary as unquestionable saviors. This selective framing blurs the line between storytelling and political messaging.

What makes Haq particularly problematic is how it packages opinion as fact. Nuanced debates around personal law, constitutional interpretation, and historical context are reduced to emotionally charged courtroom speeches designed to provoke outrage rather than understanding. In doing so, the film risks misleading audiences who may not be familiar with the real complexities of the case, effectively spreading simplified or distorted interpretations under the guise of social reform.

The timing of the film's release and its promotional narrative further amplify suspicions of political intent. Instead of encouraging informed discussion, Haq appears to capitalize on existing social tensions, reinforcing popular political talking points rather than challenging them. Social media discourse around the film shows how quickly its narrative was absorbed into polarized debates, often detached from factual accuracy.

While Haq succeeds as a dramatic film with strong performances, its broader impact is troubling. Cinema has immense power to shape public opinion, and when that power is used to advance motivated narratives and partial truths, it becomes a vehicle for disinformation rather than dialogue. Haq ultimately serves as a reminder that not all "issue-based" cinema is neutral - and viewers must watch such films with critical awareness, not blind acceptance.

title: Amazing

title: Good topic, very bad dialogue and direction

Instead of that he just focused on unnecessary scenes. All dialogues were useless no strong presence of anyone. Even the final supreme court scene was pathetic. Could have been made like movie Section 375 where each law is clearly explain everything. Could have just read the case and narrated that int he movie, simple. Hopeless work by director. Director hi bekar ho to actors kya karenge.

title: Twisting truth to serve their own good
review:
The movie claims to advocate justice and women's rights while simultaneously misrepresenting the very principles that protect those rights within the Islamic tradition. It completely portrays a distorted version of Islam that suits the narrative rather than the truth.

Highly propagated movie. Poorly researched on the rulings of triple Talaq and second marriage in islam. Clearly misleads audience in the name of equality without taking any references or context of Quranic verses and quoting caliph and scholars.

title: IQRA (To Read)
review:
I just finished watching this eye-opening masterpiece of a courtroom drama inspired by Shazia Bano's case. This drama portrays the pain, trauma, and injustice faced by thousands of Muslim women often oppressed by a conservative society, misogynistic ideology, and people who deprive women of their dignity, rights, and fundamental equality. The movie depicts the hardships of Shazia Bano's case with vivid realism. :)

I still wonder about the intensity of the trauma and hardship she and thousands of others had to go through. This case exposes the hypocrisy and irony of our society, where people emphasize finding a "decent" son- or daughter-in-law, yet struggle to build healthy relationships. Hopefully, it continues to empower laws that favor the needy, the oppressed, and the marginalized. It's high time we, the people of India, abandon those man-made conservative practices that violate the fundamental rights of human beings-whether it's triple talaq or sati pratha. 😤

The protagonist, Yami Gautam, played the role of Shazia Bano outstandingly, and the other actors delivered equally powerful performances. The plot was meaningful and emotionally enriching. 🙏

Hats off to the director, writer, actors, actresses, and all the supporting staff for creating such an impactful masterpiece for society. ^_^

Best wishes for the continued success of the film. :D.

title: Twisting truth to serve their own good!

title: Movie Review: HAQ - A Powerful Story on Triple Talaq & Women's Rights
review:
I just watched Haq and I'm still thinking about it. The film brilliantly captures the painful reality of women who suffer because of the misuse of triple talaq in society. It's emotional, bold, and thought-provoking from start to finish.

Emraan Hashmi delivers one of his finest performances-raw, controlled, and deeply impactful, and yes it is truly said by Aryan Khan "Saare Actors ki Movie ek taraf aur Emraan Hashmi ki Movie ek taraf". Yami Gautam is outstanding as always; her expressions and intensity make you feel every moment of her struggle. Their chemistry and acting elevate the entire narrative.

The film not only exposes exploitation but also encourages discussion about justice, dignity, and equality for women. The direction, dialogues, and storytelling are strong, making Haq a must-watch for anyone who believes in social change.

⭐ My Rating: 4.5/5
If you haven't watched it yet, do check it out. It's more than a movie-it's a message.

title: Layers of Emotion and Humanity
review: There's so much beneath the surface of Haq that one viewing doesn't feel enough. The script operates on multiple levels - legal, personal, spiritual - and ties them all together beautifully. Yami's portrayal of Shazia is layered with vulnerability and fire; it's impossible not to feel her journey. Emraan plays his part with quiet tension, showing both guilt and compassion. The direction keeps everything balanced and emotionally raw. It's not just a movie, it's a mirror to society's conscience.

title: Emotionally Intelligent and Deeply Human
review: What impressed me most was the emotional intelligence behind every creative choice. Instead of making the audience feel pity, the film evokes empathy. You don't watch Shazia as a victim; you experience her as a woman reclaiming agency. The dialogues are sharp but never preachy, filled with layered meaning. The cinematography frames her world with intimacy, showing how confined spaces can hold enormous emotion. It's the kind of film that grows on you long after it ends.

title: Emotion Without Excess
review: One of the hardest things in cinema is to evoke emotion without excess - Haq achieves it flawlessly. The film feels restrained yet deeply affecting, subtle yet unforgettable. Yami Gautam gives a performance that feels lived-in, full of quiet determination. Emraan Hashmi's layered portrayal adds dimension to every moral conflict. The writing, direction, and cinematography all serve the story, never overshadowing it. It's art made with heart and humility.

title: A Film That Touches the Soul
review: Haq feels like a heartfelt letter written to anyone who has ever struggled with truth. Yami Gautam gives a soul-stirring performance, filled with strength and quiet sorrow. Emraan Hashmi's portrayal brings a deep humanity to the story, his subtle shifts in expression saying more than words. The film's pace allows you to breathe and feel every emotion. It's not just cinema, it's a spiritual experience of empathy and grace.

title: Deeply Personal and Emotionally True
review: Watching Haq feels like being let into someone's private truth. Yami Gautam's performance is astonishingly intimate, as if she's living every moment instead of acting it. Emraan Hashmi's grounded performance complements her honesty. The screenplay feels deeply researched, respectful, and heartfelt. It's a story that invites you to feel, to think, and to care - all at once.

title: Beautifully Honest and Deeply Human
review:
Beautifully Honest and Deeply Human
What stands out most in Haq is its honesty. The film doesn't manipulate you; it trusts you to feel. Yami Gautam's eyes tell entire stories, her expressions layered with strength and pain. Emraan Hashmi's transformation feels authentic, making his character one of quiet redemption. The direction is gentle, yet full of conviction. It's a rare piece of cinema that feels both intimate and universal.

title: Realism Done Right
review: This is the kind of movie that doesn't rely on background music or forced tension to keep you invested. Instead, it trusts the audience to feel the emotions organically. The production design is detailed but not distracting, capturing the era and the social climate perfectly. Every supporting performance feels essential, not ornamental. By the time the verdict arrives, you realize you've been holding your breath. Haq reminds you why truth, when told well, is the most powerful narrative of all.

title: Every Word Matters
review: The dialogues in Haq are some of the most meaningful I've heard in recent cinema. They don't sound like lines - they sound like lived experience. The courtroom sequences are beautifully constructed, full of emotional tension but grounded in realism. Yami's delivery gives each line a heartbeat, and Emraan's quiet responses make every exchange feel alive. Even the silences carry weight. It's a film that proves words, when used truthfully, can change hearts.

title: A Story That Demands to Be Felt
review:
A Story That Demands to Be Felt
There are films you watch, and then there are films you feel - Haq belongs to the second kind. It's written with empathy, directed with care, and performed with rare conviction. Yami Gautam gives one of her most heartfelt portrayals, full of strength and fragility. Emraan Hashmi delivers quiet brilliance, his restraint adding weight to every scene. It's not loud, it's not flashy, it's deeply human.

title: A Rare Film That Listens
review: What makes Haq truly special is that it listens - to its characters, to the silences between words, and to the audience's intelligence. It doesn't shout for attention or try to manipulate emotion. Instead, it unfolds like a quiet confession, honest and deeply human. Yami Gautam's performance is breathtaking; she doesn't act the role, she inhabits it. Emraan Hashmi's restrained portrayal perfectly complements her strength. Every frame seems to carry weight and purpose. It's the kind of film that changes the way you think about courage.

title: A Rare Balance of Sensitivity and Strength
review:
A Rare Balance of Sensitivity and Strength
Few films manage to be sensitive and strong at the same time, but Haq achieves it effortlessly. Yami Gautam's portrayal is raw yet composed, filled with a rare emotional intelligence. Emraan Hashmi brings sincerity to his character, portraying moral conflict with subtle precision. The writing feels deeply researched and humane. It's a film that doesn't just tell a story - it honors it.

title: The Power of Understatement
review: Not many films dare to be understated, but Haq proves how powerful it can be. The storytelling is simple, yet it hits you deeply. Yami Gautam embodies her role with poise and precision, never overstating emotion. Emraan Hashmi's subtle expressions speak volumes. The film's emotional control is its greatest strength. It's the kind of cinema that earns silence, not applause, at the end.

title: A Film That Breathes Humanity
review: Few movies today breathe the way Haq does. It allows space for thought, silence, and emotion to coexist. Yami Gautam delivers a performance of great empathy and grace. Emraan Hashmi supports her beautifully, giving humanity to every line he delivers. The cinematography feels organic, the world believable. It's a simple yet profound reminder of the beauty of sincerity.

title: Grace, Grit, and Greatness
review: I found myself deeply moved by how gracefully Haq handles such complex themes. It never talks down to the audience; it invites you to think and feel. The pacing is deliberate, almost meditative, and that gives weight to every emotion. Yami proves again that she is one of the finest actors working today, while Emraan gives perhaps his most mature performance. The direction deserves special praise for never resorting to melodrama. This is brave, beautiful filmmaking at its best.

title: Real Characters, Real Stories
review: What makes Haq special is its realism. The people feel real, their choices understandable, their pain relatable. Yami Gautam shines, portraying both strength and exhaustion with honesty. Emraan Hashmi gives one of his most humane performances, deeply grounded and believable. The film avoids clichés, choosing subtlety over spectacle. It's storytelling that respects both its subject and its audience.

title: Heartfelt and Courageous Storytelling
review:
Heartfelt and Courageous Storytelling
Haq stands tall because it dares to be honest. It doesn't chase commercial appeal, it seeks emotional truth. Yami Gautam is absolutely remarkable, breathing life into a character caught between vulnerability and strength. Emraan Hashmi gives a performance that's deeply human, neither black nor white. The film's courage lies in its simplicity, and that's what makes it unforgettable.

title: The Kind of Cinema We Need More Of
review: Watching Haq reminded me of why I fell in love with cinema in the first place. It's storytelling that feels pure - driven by emotion, not ego. The film handles complex themes with maturity and grace, never simplifying them for comfort. Yami Gautam gives a performance that's both fierce and fragile, making you root for her from the start. Emraan Hashmi's understated presence adds balance and realism. It's not just a film, it's a quiet revolution in empathy.

title: A Poem in Motion
review:
A Poem in Motion
Haq unfolds like a poem, every line filled with meaning, every pause intentional. Yami Gautam performs with poetry in her expressions, creating a symphony of quiet strength. Emraan Hashmi complements her beautifully, his calm demeanor anchoring the story. The cinematography feels like brushstrokes on canvas, soft yet powerful. By the end, you don't just remember the film, you feel like you've lived inside it.

title: A Beautiful Reflection of Real Life
review:
A Beautiful Reflection of Real Life
Haq feels like a reflection rather than a performance. The emotions are so genuine that it's easy to forget you're watching actors. Yami Gautam's portrayal of Shazia is deeply compassionate and moving. Emraan Hashmi brings subtle strength to his role, revealing his character's layers with care. The direction avoids clichés, letting truth be the driving force. It's a film that mirrors the world as it should be seen, with empathy.

title: Emotion That Feels Lived, Not Performed
review: Every frame of Haq feels authentic, as though the actors aren't performing but living those moments. Yami Gautam's ability to communicate deep emotion through silence is unmatched. Emraan Hashmi's restraint adds depth, his eyes revealing more than words could. The writing is compassionate, treating its characters with dignity. This is cinema that moves you without ever trying to.

title: A Testament to Thoughtful Filmmaking
review: Haq is proof that storytelling still matters. Every element of the film, from the performances to the writing, feels intentional and thoughtful. Yami Gautam's Shazia carries both grace and fire, making you feel every ounce of her struggle. Emraan Hashmi delivers a performance full of nuance and empathy. The film's pace is patient, letting you sit with the truth instead of rushing through it. It's intelligent, heartfelt cinema at its best.

title: When Truth Finds Its Voice
review:
When Truth Finds Its Voice
Haq is about finding your voice when silence becomes unbearable. It tells this journey with sensitivity and grace. Yami Gautam is outstanding, portraying Shazia with restraint and fire in equal measure. Emraan Hashmi's performance complements hers, full of compassion and honesty. The film's direction never wavers, always rooted in truth. It's storytelling at its purest - sincere, emotional, and unforgettable.

title: A Rare Emotional Honesty
review:
A Rare Emotional Honesty
Haq connects because it never hides behind dramatics. The emotions are genuine, the storytelling effortless. Yami Gautam delivers a performance filled with quiet honesty, every gesture and pause feels real. Emraan Hashmi supports her beautifully, portraying moral struggle with dignity. The cinematography is simple yet powerful, giving space for emotion to breathe. It's rare to find such truth in cinema today, and Haq offers it in abundance.

title: Emotionally Intelligent and Beautifully Crafted
review: Haq is a thinking person's film. It trusts the audience's intelligence and emotional depth. Yami Gautam is simply extraordinary, balancing grace and grit with seamless ease. Emraan Hashmi's role is played with controlled brilliance, subtle yet unforgettable. The direction captures the tension between faith and justice without sensationalism. It's cinema that respects your heart and mind equally.

title: Power in Simplicity
review: There's something incredibly moving about how simple Haq feels on the surface and how profound it becomes underneath. The dialogue, the framing, the silences - everything feels deliberate yet natural. Yami Gautam transforms restraint into power; she says more with her eyes than others could with monologues. Emraan Hashmi delivers his best performance in years, full of control and compassion. It's cinema that trusts intelligence over spectacle, and that's what makes it unforgettable.

title: Bold Yet Balanced Storytelling
review: Few films have the courage to take on sensitive issues without falling into the trap of one-sidedness. Haq does it with intelligence and fairness. The writing neither panders nor provokes; it simply tells a human story. Yami's performance is extraordinary - calm, emotional, unbreakable. Emraan brings an unexpected tenderness to a role that could have been cold. The film is visually understated, emotionally rich, and morally resonant. A quiet triumph.

title: A Masterpiece of Moderation
review: Haq proves that less truly can be more. The film's greatest strength is its restraint - in direction, performance, and emotion. The courtroom sequences feel alive, but never theatrical. Yami Gautam owns every frame, making Shazia's pain and power equally believable. Emraan delivers perhaps the most dignified performance of his career. There's integrity in every choice the film makes, and that integrity makes it unforgettable.

title: Yami & Vishal Scores!! Must Watch!!
review:
Loosely based on the powerful true story of Shah Bano, Haq is a cinematic triumph that excels across all departments.

Directed by Suparn Verma and penned by Reshu Nath, the film is beautifully executed, offering a seamless blend of poignant emotions and sharp dialogue.

Yami Gautam delivers an impeccable performance as Shazi Bano; she doesn't just act-she truly inhabits the character. Emraan Hashmi provides strong support, alongside notable turns by Danish Husain, Aseem Hattangady, Sheeba Chaddha, and Vartika Singh.

The soul of the film, however, lies in Vishal Mishra's background score and music.

It is truly unfortunate that such a well-reviewed film underperformed at the box office.

With a performance this commanding, Yami Gautam may very well be a frontrunner for the National Awards this year.

title: One of the best movie I've ever seen
review:
As a Muslim, I 100% agree with the message of this movie. Haq is a bold, honest, and much-needed film that speaks about truth, justice, and standing up for what is right without fear. It beautifully highlights how media narratives and social pressure can distort reality, and how important it is to question falsehood with courage and integrity.

Yami Gautam absolutely nailed it 👏. Her performance was intense, fearless, and deeply convincing. She carried the film with grace and strength, delivering every scene with powerful emotions and clarity. This is easily one of her finest performances to date.

The movie forces viewers to think beyond propaganda and stereotypes and encourages fairness, balance, and humanity. Whether you agree or disagree with the topic, Haq deserves to be watched for its strong storytelling, impactful performances, and honest message.

Highly recommended for anyone who believes in truth, justice, and freedom of thought.

title: A Powerful, Honest & Fearless Film - Yami Gautam at Her Absolute Best
review:
Haq stands out as a rare film that combines strong storytelling with social responsibility. What truly elevates the movie is Yami Gautam's phenomenal performance controlled, dignified, and deeply impactful. Her dialogue delivery deserves special appreciation, especially the way she articulates sensitive arguments with clarity and conviction.

The film deserves credit for portraying Sharia law in its correct context, something that is often misunderstood and misrepresented. The narrative calmly but firmly addresses common misconceptions around Triple Talaq and the concept of having four wives, without sensationalism or bias. Instead of preaching, the film educates allowing facts, logic, and faith-based references to speak for themselves.

A standout aspect is how Yami delivers Quranic verses along with their meanings, making them accessible and relevant even to viewers unfamiliar with Islamic jurisprudence. Her performance never feels forced; she justifies every word with grace, intelligence, and emotional depth. This is not just acting-it is embodiment.

Kudos to the writer and director for handling such a sensitive subject with maturity, balance, and courage. The film neither vilifies nor glorifies but presents reality with nuance and respect.

Overall, Haq is a brave, thought-provoking film anchored by Yami Gautam's career-best performance. A must-watch for those who appreciate meaningful cinema that challenges stereotypes and encourages informed understanding.

title: Yami Gautam Dhar Show.
review:
Well, I am happy that this film is actually based on true events and inspired by a real court case. Otherwise, this might have been labeled as a propaganda film by critics.

The film pays close attention to detail and is both religiously and legally accurate. Apart from that, it addresses a major issue present in almost all religions-people often refuse to read the actual texts and instead blindly believe whatever a priest or the person in charge of a temple, mosque, or church tells them.

Apart from the story and screenplay, the one thing that stands out the most is the acting, especially by the main lead, Yami Gautam Dhar. She has played her role magnificently. Her final courtroom speech stole the show, and she outshone every other major actress in Bollywood. I would be surprised if she does not receive a National Award, a Filmfare Award, or an IIFA Award for this performance. That said, I already know that Filmfare and IIFA awards are not always credible when it comes to merit. For example, Shah Rukh Khan winning a National Award for Jawan. If that can happen, I am sure Yami Gautam will receive a National Award for this role.

Emraan Hashmi has improved tremendously, and the film relies heavily on his shoulders. He justified his role perfectly. He did not overact; instead, he found the perfect tone and demeanor for the character and portrayed it beautifully. The supporting role played by Emraan Hashmi's second wife was also performed very well. Every character makes a strong impact on the film, but as mentioned earlier, Yami Gautam Dhar truly steals the show.

title: Good on
review:
Good on. Have a good time. Powerful, relevant story & social relevance: The film, inspired by the landmark Shah Bano case, deals with divorce, triple-talaq, women's rights and justice under law. It turns a personal tragedy into a broader commentary on faith, law and women's dignity - a subject that remains deeply relevant in today's India.

Strong performances (especially the lead actress): The lead performance by Yami Gautam Dhar is widely praised - understated yet powerful, conveying pain, despair, dignity and resolve with subtlety rather than melodrama. The male lead, Emraan Hashmi, is also lauded for portraying a morally ambiguous character with restraint (not a cardboard villain).

Balanced tone & direction - not sensationalist: The film avoids caricaturing religion or resorting to melodramatic outrage. Instead, the director - Suparn S Varma - opts for a mature, nuanced take: showing how faith, law and patriarchy intersect without demonizing religion wholesale.

Sensitive writing & screenplay: According to many critics, the writing - dialogues, screenplay - brings authenticity, especially in capturing the social milieu of 1970s-80s North India. The narrative stays grounded, personal, human.

title: Amazing movie. Loved to see Emran Hashmi.
review: Man, I am still buzzing after watching Haq because it's not just a movie; it's the massive comeback for Emraan Hashmi that we've all been starving for. Honestly, it's been far too long since we saw him sink his teeth into a role with this much meat on the bone, and seeing him play Advocate Abbas Khan felt like a total homecoming for those of us who knew he always had this level of depth in him. The plot is absolutely gripping, pulling from the real-life intensity of the Shah Bano case, but it handles the legal and social weight with so much grit that you're hooked from the very first frame. I loved how the movie didn't just make him a typical hero; he's sharp, ego-driven, and morally complex, which is exactly the "gray" space where Emraan shines the brightest. Watching the story evolve from a messy domestic fallout into this huge national constitutional battle was a total thrill, and the courtroom face-offs were written with such sharp, biting dialogue that you could feel the tension in the theater. There's a specific kind of swagger Emraan brings back to the screen here, but it's evolved now-it's more restrained, more intellectual, and his silences actually say more than most actors' monologues. Yami Gautam is incredible as Shazia, and the way their relationship crumbles under the weight of tradition and law is just heartbreakingly well-done. I was so impressed by how the director captured the 80s vibe without making it feel like a caricature; it felt lived-in, dusty, and real. It's a relief to finally see a script that respects the audience's intelligence and gives an actor of Emraan's caliber the room to breathe and dominate the screen again. Every time he stepped into that courtroom, the energy shifted, and I found myself rooting for the justice of the story even while being mesmerized by his character's flaws.
